findings.description: get() calls load() on every invocation, and require()/feature_enabled() funnel through get(), so each accessor re-reads the file and re-parses YAML and re-scans the entire environ with zero caching; a request reading N config values pays N disk reads plus N YAML parses on a hot path.

load() performs full disk I/O (os.path.exists + open + yaml.load via _read_file) plus a complete os.environ walk on every call with no memoization; fix by parsing once in __init__ or caching load() (cached_property/lru_cache) so accessors hit an in-memory dict.

yaml.load uses PyYAML's slow pure-Python default loader; use yaml.safe_load or Loader=CSafeLoader for the C-accelerated path (the missing SafeLoader is also an RCE risk — defer security severity to critic-security), and this parse cost compounds with the no-caching issue.

_read_env() recomputes the full APP_* environ overlay (iterating all of os.environ and coercing each match) on every load() even though environment variables are effectively immutable for the process lifetime — redundant work that the load()-level caching fix subsumes.
os.path.exists() followed by open() double-stats the file; a single try/open/except FileNotFoundError avoids the extra syscall — negligible versus the no-caching problem, flagged for completeness.

dict(DEFAULTS) is a shallow copy so merged['feature_flags'] aliases the mutable module-global default dict; a caller mutating returned flags corrupts defaults for all future loads — correctness landmine, defer detail to critic-logic.
